description: Routing scenarios for ppds-query: the SQL->FetchXML engine (ppds query sql / ppds_query_sql) is the default surface for reading, counting, joining, and aggregating Dataverse records; raw FetchXML routes to ppds query fetch / ppds_query_fetch; plans and transpilation route to ppds query explain / --show-fetchxml; guarded DML uses --dry-run then --confirm. The engine paginates, so there is NO --limit/--max-rows flag on `ppds query sql` (row capping is --top, alias -t) and MySQL/Postgres LIMIT is unsupported (use TOP). Never route to pac or raw Web API. Every asserted command and flag is copied verbatim from skills/ppds-query/references/cli-query.md and sql-dialect.md; ppds_* MCP names are from skills/ppds-core/references/mcp-tools.md.

scenarios.prompt: How many active account records are in my Dataverse environment right now? I just need the total, not the rows.

assertions.rubric: Routes the count to the SQL->FetchXML engine (`ppds query sql` or ppds_query_sql) using a COUNT(*) aggregate and does not invent a --limit flag — the engine paginates and accurate counting is a COUNT query, not a row fetch.

scenarios.prompt: For each account owner, give me the owner and how many open accounts they have, but only show owners with more than 10. Join in the owning user's name if you can.

assertions.rubric: Expresses the per-owner grouped count with HAVING as a single SQL statement through `ppds query sql` (the engine transpiles JOIN/GROUP BY/HAVING to FetchXML) rather than hand-writing FetchXML or building an OData $apply aggregation.

scenarios.prompt: Show me the 5 contacts that were created most recently, newest first.

assertions.rubric: Caps the result with SQL `TOP 5` (or the real --top/-t flag) and explicitly avoids LIMIT and any --limit/--max-rows flag, which do not exist on this engine.

scenarios.prompt: In our Production org, update every contact whose job title is 'Developer' to 'Senior Developer'. There are a few thousand of them. Go ahead and run it.

assertions.rubric: Treats the Production UPDATE as guarded: names the target environment, previews with `ppds query sql ... --dry-run` and shows the row estimate, and only then executes with --confirm after explicit human approval — it never bypasses the guard with --no-limit, never invents --force, and never silently runs the update.

scenarios.prompt: A SQL query I'm running against Dataverse is returning weird results. I want to see exactly what FetchXML it gets turned into, without actually running it.

assertions.rubric: Surfaces the transpilation/plan via `ppds query sql --show-fetchxml` (or `ppds query explain`) so the user sees the FetchXML without executing the query, rather than guessing or running the query.
